headline: A Frog's Secret Weapon: How Some Populations Beat a Deadly Fungus
meta_description: Scientists discovered why some frog populations survive a devastating fungus while others collapse—it comes down to timing and tadpole immunity.
summary_short: Researchers found that amphibian populations recovering from a deadly fungus develop immune defenses as tadpoles, giving them protection before adulthood makes them vuln…
body:
Around four lakes in the Pyrenees of France and Spain, something unexpected happened. While one midwife toad population nearly vanished, three others rebounded—despite the same deadly fungus still lingering in the water.

Scientists from University College London finally understood why. The difference came down to timing.

The chytrid fungus Batrachochytrium dendrobatidis attacks adult frogs by damaging their keratinized skin, disrupting their ability to regulate water and minerals. Tadpoles, with their softer skin, remain largely safe. But researchers discovered that toads from recovering populations developed powerful immune peptides—natural antimicrobial chemicals—while still tadpoles. By the time they matured and became vulnerable, their defenses were already battle-ready.

Toads from the struggling population never got this head start, leaving them defenseless when infection struck.

The study uncovered something even more remarkable: using advanced mass spectrometry, researchers identified 1,152 antimicrobial peptides in the toads' skin. Only seven had been documented before. This vast hidden arsenal could eventually inspire new human medicines, especially as antimicrobial resistance grows increasingly troubling.

The findings, published in Nature Chemical Biology, prove that species devastated by disease aren't necessarily doomed. They possess the tools to recover—it just depends on when those tools develop.
